the_deacon_personal_character.concerns_about_deficiencies_in_personal_:
Overall, Michael exhibits a strong and well-rounded Christian character, particularly in areas of humility, faithfulness, dependability, and a genuine servant heart. That said, there are a few areas that I believe warrant attention, not because they are glaring deficiencies, but because growth here would significantly enhance his long-term effectiveness in the diaconal role.

1. BALANCE BETWEEN ENERGETIC SERVICE AND DISCIPLINE
Michael is highly energetic and eager to serve, often being one of the first to volunteer for hospital visits, benevolence calls, and special outreach events. However, his enthusiasm sometimes outpaces his personal organizational discipline. For example, there have been a few occasions this past year when he double-booked himself for a Sunday afternoon shut-in visit and an evening youth service project. In both cases, everything ultimately got covered, but it required last-minute reshuffling and leaning on other volunteers. This suggests that while his heart is absolutely in the right place, he would benefit from more deliberate calendaring, clearer boundaries, and occasional willingness to say “no” to preserve quality over quantity.

2. TENDENCY TO AVOID CONFRONTATION
Michael is deeply empathetic and sensitive to others’ feelings, which is a strength in pastoral care. However, that same sensitivity sometimes makes him hesitant to address difficult issues directly. In one instance, he became aware that a benevolence recipient was misrepresenting their financial situation to the church. Instead of bringing the concern to me promptly, he tried for several weeks to "drop hints" and gently redirect the person, hoping they would correct course on their own. By the time he brought the situation to me, the pattern had become more entrenched, and it was harder to address. This pattern is not rooted in dishonesty but rather in a fear of hurting or disappointing people.

3. GROWTH IN DISCERNMENT AND WISDOM UNDER PRESSURE
Michael’s judgment is generally sound, especially when he has time to think and pray through a situation. Under time pressure, though, he can lean more on his emotions than on careful discernment. For example, during a particularly intense week last fall, he approved a small, one-time gas card for a walk-in request without doing the usual verification we ask for (basic ID and a quick needs assessment). It turned out fine in that specific case, but afterward he acknowledged that he skipped steps out of a desire to “just solve the problem quickly” and move on to his next visit. We have since talked about the importance of consistent processes, especially in benevolence, both for fairness and for wise stewardship.

4. SERIOUSNESS VS. LIGHTHEARTEDNESS
Michael is cheerful, encouraging, and well-liked, especially among our seniors and young families. Occasionally, though, his lightheartedness leads him to make a joke or light comment at a moment that calls for greater gravity. One example: during a family meeting following a difficult cancer diagnosis, he made a well-intentioned, humorous remark to break the tension. The family did not take offense, but afterward he himself recognized that the timing was not ideal and that it would have been better simply to sit in silence and allow the family’s grief to be fully expressed. We have debriefed that situation, and he has been more attentive to reading the room in subsequent visits.

5. FOLLOW-THROUGH ON PERSONAL SELF-CARE
Michael is highly sacrificial with his time and energy but doesn’t always extend the same care to his own spiritual and physical well-being. He is faithful in worship and Bible study, but when the schedule gets crowded, he tends to cut back on rest, exercise, and unhurried time with his family. Earlier this winter he went nearly three weeks without taking his scheduled day off, and it showed in his fatigue and decreased patience with minor frustrations. After a pastoral check-in, he agreed to be more intentional about observing a weekly Sabbath day and is now doing better in this area, though it remains a growth edge.

In summary, none of these concerns rise to the level of questioning his suitability for diaconal ministry. Rather, they are areas for ongoing growth and mentoring. Michael is teachable, open to feedback, and genuinely desires to mature. With continued guidance, especially in the areas of discernment under pressure, healthy boundaries, and comfort with loving confrontation, I am confident he will continue to develop into an even more effective and well-balanced servant of Christ and His people.

relationship_between_supervising_pastor_.description_of_relationship_between_supe:
I have served as Michael Carlson’s supervising pastor at Grace Lutheran Church in Lansing, Michigan, for the past three and a half years. During this time, our relationship has developed from a basic supervisory dynamic into a genuine partnership in ministry, marked by open communication, mutual respect, and a shared commitment to serving Christ and His people.

OPENNESS AND GUIDABILITY
From the beginning, Michael has shown a high degree of openness to feedback and a willingness to learn. When he first began serving as a deacon, we established a rhythm of meeting every other Tuesday morning for 60–90 minutes. In those meetings, we review his current cases (hospital visits, shut-in calls, benevolence situations), debrief any difficult conversations, and talk through upcoming responsibilities. Michael consistently comes prepared with notes, questions, and specific situations where he wants input.

A concrete example: last fall, Michael visited a member who was estranged from her adult son. The conversation became emotional, and Michael wasn’t sure afterward whether he had said too much or too little. At our next supervision meeting, he brought a detailed summary of the visit, including what he said, how she responded, and what he was internally feeling at the time. He specifically asked, “If you had been in my shoes, what would you have done differently?” That level of self-reflection and teachability is typical of him.

LOYALTY AND WILLINGNESS
Michael has demonstrated loyalty both to the congregation and to the pastoral office. He does not undermine leadership decisions, even when he might personally have done something differently. When we implemented a new benevolence policy that added a bit more structure and documentation, he initially found it cumbersome, but he voiced his concerns privately with me rather than complaining to volunteers or members. Once he understood the rationale (fairness, accountability, and long-term sustainability), he became an advocate for the changes and helped explain them patiently to those we serve.

He is also consistently willing to step in where needed. For instance, when I was unexpectedly called out of town for a family emergency last year, Michael quietly rearranged his own work schedule to cover several of my regular shut-in communions, coordinate rides for two members to their medical appointments, and organize a last-minute prayer gathering for the congregation. He did all of this without making it about himself or seeking recognition.

CONSIDERATION AND RESPECT
Interpersonally, Michael is considerate and respectful. In our conversations, he does not interrupt, listens carefully, and takes notes. When we disagree on a pastoral approach (for example, on how quickly to move toward church discipline in a particular case), he states his perspective honestly but always with deference to the pastoral office and to Scripture. He has never pushed an agenda or tried to insert himself into decisions that rightly belong to the pastor and elders.

He is also respectful of boundaries. If something clearly falls into the realm of pastoral authority (e.g., questions about communion fellowship, formal church discipline, or doctrinal concerns), he will bring it to me rather than attempting to handle it independently. At the same time, he is not overly timid; he understands the scope of his diaconal responsibilities and carries them out confidently within that framework.

FREQUENCY AND QUALITY OF COMMUNICATION
We maintain regular, structured communication as well as informal check-ins. Formally, we meet twice a month for supervision, and Michael sends me a brief written summary of his pastoral care contacts every Friday, noting any urgent concerns or follow-up needs. Informally, we often talk briefly after worship on Sundays and occasionally by phone or text during the week if a time-sensitive situation arises (e.g., a member being taken to the hospital, a death in the congregation, or a pressing benevolence need).

The quality of our communication is generally very good. Michael is honest about when something feels beyond his experience or emotional capacity. A notable example occurred when he was walking with a family through the end-of-life process for an elderly parent. After several weeks of increasingly intense visits, he came to me and said, “I’m starting to feel emotionally overwhelmed, and I’m worried it might affect my ability to be fully present for them. Could you take the lead on the next couple of visits while I stay involved in a supporting role?” That level of self-awareness and transparency has allowed us to minister more effectively as a team.

EVALUATION AND ONGOING DEVELOPMENT
Our relationship includes ongoing evaluation, both formal and informal. At least twice a year, we set aside part of a supervision meeting specifically to review Michael’s growth, revisit earlier goals, and identify new areas for development. Topics we have addressed include:
- Improving his note-taking and documentation after visits so that important details are not lost.
- Learning to set healthier boundaries around his time off and family commitments.
- Growing in comfort with appropriate, loving confrontation when a situation requires it.
- Developing greater discernment in complex benevolence cases where multiple issues (addiction, mental health, family dynamics) are at play.

Michael receives this evaluation in a mature, non-defensive way. He often follows up a few weeks later with observations about how the changes are going, and he is quick to acknowledge where he still struggles. I have seen clear growth in him year over year, especially in his ability to pace himself, to ask for help when needed, and to think theologically about the situations he encounters.

OVERALL ASSESSMENT OF THE RELATIONSHIP
In summary, my relationship with Michael as his supervising pastor is healthy, collaborative, and marked by trust. I am comfortable delegating significant responsibilities to him within the bounds of the diaconal role, knowing that he will both carry them out faithfully and keep me appropriately informed. I experience him as:
- Open and guidable, not set in his ways.
- Loyal to the congregation, the pastoral office, and the wider church.
- Willing to serve wherever needed, while increasingly attentive to healthy limits.
- Considerate and respectful in both speech and conduct.
- Communicative, honest, and transparent, especially when facing difficult situations.

I believe our working relationship provides a solid foundation for his continued growth and service as a deacon in the Michigan District, and I am grateful for the privilege of mentoring him in this season of his ministry.
